response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend maybe an hour on social media daily, mostly scrolling through photography pages and gaming communities, and honestly the fake content situation is exhausting. I work with my hands all day as a carpenter in Bari, building real things with real wood, so when I see obviously fabricated stories or doctored images online, it just irritates me. People share the most ridiculous stuff without even thinking about it. I have seen posts claiming miracle cures or conspiracy theories about everything from vaccines to politics, and half my feed sometimes feels like complete nonsense. The thing is, I am not great at staying organized or checking every single source myself, I will admit that. When you are tired after a long shift and just want to unwind, you do not always have the energy to fact-check everything. But I have learned to trust my gut more. If something seems too outrageous or perfectly designed to make you angry, it probably is fake. I try to pause before sharing anything, even though I am not the most careful person generally. What really gets me is how this fake content affects my kid. I want to raise my daughter to think critically, not just believe whatever pops up on a screen. So even though I am not the most social person and tend to keep to myself, I do make an effort to talk to her about what is real and what is not when she sees me on my phone. My approach is pretty straightforward and maybe a bit blunt. I just ignore most of the obvious garbage, avoid engaging with posts that seem designed to provoke fights, and stick to communities focused on actual interests like woodworking techniques or garden photography. I do not have patience for drama or people spreading misinformation deliberately. Sometimes I think platforms should do more to filter this stuff out, but honestly I also think people need to take more personal responsibility. Not everything needs to be shared or believed just because it confirms what you already think. In my limited time online, I would rather see genuine content from real people than algorithm-generated outrage bait.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 30-year-old female carpenter with lower secondary education, living in Bari, Italy. Key traits influencing the response: low conscientiousness (admits to not being organized or thorough in checking sources), low agreeableness (blunt, direct tone, low patience for nonsense), low extraversion (keeps to herself, not highly social), high openness (willing to consider different perspectives, interested in various hobbies), low neuroticism (stable, not anxious about the issue), and depression (mentions exhaustion, limited energy). Her occupation as a carpenter provides a practical, hands-on worldview that contrasts with digital fakery. She has one child, which motivates concern about media literacy. Limited daily social media use (one hour) means selective engagement. Her hobbies (videogames, photography, gardening) inform which communities she follows. The tone is direct, somewhat cynical, practical, and unpretentious, reflecting her working-class background, limited formal education, and low agreeableness. She focuses on practical strategies rather than systematic approaches, consistent with low conscientiousness.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, practical, slightly cynical, unpretentious, working-class perspective, straightforward
